sections.description: Keep payment-related systems and connections understood and limited.

questions.question: Have you documented how cardholder data flows (for example, from your site to your payment provider) and which systems could touch it?
questions.help_text: A simple diagram or written flow is enough if it matches how checkout really works.

sections.description: Systems are set up safely and not left on unsafe defaults.

questions.question: Have you changed or disabled vendor default passwords and risky default settings on systems involved in your payment flow (for example, web servers or admin consoles)?
questions.help_text: Applies to any in-scope system—not usually the card brands’ hosted payment page itself.

sections.description: Limit what card data you keep and where it lives.

questions.question: Do you avoid storing full card numbers, magnetic stripe data, or card security codes after authorization on your systems or with your software?
questions.help_text: SAQ A assumes checkout is fully outsourced; if you store any PAN, this SAQ may not fit.

questions.question: Do you ensure sensitive authentication data (such as full magnetic stripe or chip data) is never stored after authorization?
questions.help_text: Even briefly storing this data is out of scope for a typical SAQ A setup. If you keep any account data elsewhere, you should also limit retention and dispose of it securely.

sections.description: Protect card data when it moves across open networks.

questions.question: Do you use strong encryption when cardholder data is sent over open or public networks (for example, between your customer and your payment provider)?
questions.help_text: Your PSP usually handles this; confirm your integration does not weaken it.

questions.question: If you manage any TLS certificates or keys for payment pages or redirects, are they current, trusted, and protected from unauthorized change?
questions.help_text: Choose Not Applicable if your PSP hosts all payment pages and you never touch certificates for them.

sections.description: Keep software up to date and changes controlled.

questions.question: Do you apply security patches to in-scope systems (for example, CMS, plugins, or servers) on a regular schedule?
questions.help_text: Focus on systems that could change what the customer sees or where payment data goes.

sections.description: Limit who can reach systems and data related to payments.

questions.question: Is access to systems that could change your checkout or payment integration limited to people who need it for their job?
questions.help_text: Includes hosting, DNS, e-commerce admin, and analytics that touch payment pages.

questions.question: Do you review user and administrator accounts at least periodically and remove people who no longer need access?
questions.help_text: Even a simple quarterly or annual review counts if it is documented.

sections.description: Verify identity before granting access to sensitive systems.

questions.question: Does each person use their own account (not shared logins) for admin or payment-related systems?
questions.help_text: Shared “admin” passwords are a common gap.

questions.question: Do you require multi-factor authentication or other strong controls for remote access into systems that could affect your payment page or redirect?
questions.help_text: Applies when staff connect from outside your network into in-scope systems.

sections.description: Record and review activity that could show security problems.

questions.question: Do you prevent full card numbers from appearing in application or security logs?
questions.help_text: Truncation, masking, or not logging payment fields at all.

questions.question: Do you maintain an information security policy that covers protection of payment-related systems and data?
questions.help_text: Can be a short policy if it is real and followed.

questions.question: Do you keep a list of service providers that handle payments or security for you, and confirm they meet PCI expectations for their role?
questions.help_text: Includes your payment gateway, hosting, and any party with access to payment flow.

questions.question: Do people with access to payment-related systems receive security awareness training (for example, phishing and password hygiene)?
questions.help_text: Annual training or onboarding plus refreshers is typical.
